humanities_role_explanation: Evaluates LLM translation quality specifically on classical Islamic scholarly literature including the Quran, Hadith, Tafseer, and Islamic Jurisprudence.
benchmark_modalities_explanation: Processes Arabic text from the Al-Shamela digital library and evaluates the generated English translation text.
analytical_tasks_explanation: Translating classical Arabic religious literature into English and assessing semantic preservation through back-translation and embedding-based cosine similarity analysis.

general_purpose_benchmark.note: The paper does not present a general-purpose benchmark, but rather an application-specific translation pipeline and evaluation framework for Islamic texts.

evaluates_generative_llm.note: Evaluates commercial models (GPT-4o-mini, GPT-4o, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, Claude 3.5 Haiku, Claude 3.0/3.5 Haiku) and open-source models (Llama 3.2, Qwen 2.5, Silma, Jais, Mistral).

llm_is_primary_system.note: Generative LLMs are the primary systems evaluated for both the forward translation and back-translation verification tasks.

arts_humanities_data.note: Evaluates on Islamic literature (Religious Studies, Philosophy, and Literature).

quantitative_metrics.note: Uses vector embedding cosine similarity scores to quantitatively validate translation quality.

english_language.note: The paper is written in English and translates the Arabic corpus into English.

summary: This paper presents an automated pipeline using LLMs (primarily GPT-4o-mini) to translate over 250,000 pages of classical Islamic scholarly literature from the Al-Shamela library into English. It introduces a translation validation method that uses LLMs to back-translate the text into Arabic and evaluates semantic similarity using vector embedding cosine similarity.
key_findings: 71% of the OpenAI-based back-translations and 86% of the Anthropic-based back-translations achieved acceptable quality (cosine similarity score >= 0.7) compared to the original Arabic source, showing that low-cost LLMs can reliably translate complex religious literature.
